1: Elohim, emet (truth, disp 2), YHWH, and tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) are all flagged -- those who swear by the name of the LORD and invoke the God of Israel -- but not in truth or righteousness. Israel swears by the divine name (YHWH) and invokes Elohim of Israel, but the swearing is not be-emet u-vetsedaqah (in truth and in righteousness). The MKT renders emet as truth and tsedaqah as righteousness -- the paired terms for the quality of covenant speech that Israel lacks while using the correct formulae.
2: Elohim, YHWH, and qodesh (holiness, disp 2) are flagged -- they call themselves after the holy city (ir haqodesh: the city of holiness) and claim to rest on the God of Israel. The divine name YHWH of hosts is his name. The irony: the holy city's inhabitants use the city's holiness as a credential while not sharing its holiness. The MKT's holy city preserves ir haqodesh -- the holiness attributed to the city, not to the people claiming it.
3: The former things declared long ago went out from YHWH's mouth and were proclaimed; then suddenly the action came, and it happened. The pattern of prediction-then-fulfillment is YHWH's authentication method: the historical track record of announced-and-fulfilled events is the evidence that the same LORD who acted in the past is making new announcements that will also be fulfilled.
4: Because I knew you are stubborn -- iron neck muscle, bronze forehead. The anatomical metaphors for stubbornness: the neck that will not bow (iron-sinewed) and the forehead that will not show shame (bronze-faced). The advance declaration of judgment was given because of the known stubbornness: if YHWH had waited to declare until the event, the stubborn people would have said their idols did it.
5: I announced it from long ago, before it happened, so that you could not say, My idol did it; my carved image or cast idol commanded these things. The divine preemptive announcement of history is specifically designed to prevent idol-credit: the prophecy-before-fulfillment method is a form of copyright marking -- the announcement stakes the claim to the event before it happens, so no alternative claimant can appropriate the credit afterward.
6: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- you have heard; now look at all this; and will you not declare it? The witness-obligation follows the hearing: after YHWH's prophetic declaration is confirmed by events, the obligation is to declare it to others. From now on I announce new things, hidden things that you have not known. The hidden things (netsuroth: kept/guarded things) are in reserve -- YHWH has not yet declared everything; a store of new announcements remains.
7: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- you cannot say, Yes, I knew them. The new things are being created now, not from long ago; before today you had never heard of them. The radical newness prevents Israel's claim to prior knowledge. The refrain you cannot say I already knew this is the anti-self-congratulation clause: the new things belong to YHWH's initiative alone, not to Israel's perceptiveness.
8: Yada is flagged twice -- you have not heard, you have not known; from of old your ear has not been opened. The double yada-negatives (not heard, not known) describe habitual non-perception. For I knew (yada) that you would surely betray -- the divine knowledge of human faithlessness precedes and pre-accommodates Israel's repeated failure. The MKT's from of old your ear has not been opened renders the chronic failure of covenant hearing.
9: For the sake of my name I hold back my anger; for my honor (tehillati: my praise/glory) I restrain it, so that I do not cut you off. The divine self-restraint is motivated by the divine name: cutting off Israel would compromise the LORD's testimony before the nations. The restraint of judgment is not because Israel deserves mercy but because YHWH's name and testimony are at stake -- the same motivation given for the wilderness-rescue in Ezekiel 20:9,14,22.
10: I have refined you, but not as silver is refined -- I have tested you in the furnace of affliction. The refinement metaphor with a qualification: not as silver (completely removed from dross) but in the furnace of affliction (kur oni: the smelting pot of poverty/affliction). The partial character of the refining is honest: Israel has been refined but is not yet fully refined. The furnace of affliction is the Babylonian exile.
11: Kavod (glory, disp 2) is flagged -- For my own sake, for my own sake, I act -- for how could my name be profaned? My glory I will not share with another. The double lema'ani lema'ani (for my own sake, for my own sake) is the most emphatic possible self-reference: the motivation for acting is entirely internal to YHWH. The refusal to share kavod (glory) echoes Isaiah 42:8: I am the LORD, I will not give my glory to another. The MKT preserves the double repetition that intensifies the divine self-motivation.
12: Listen to me, O Jacob, and Israel, whom I called: I am he; I am the first; I am also the last. The I-am formula in triplicate: ani hu (I am he), ani rishon (I am first), ani acharon (I am also the last). Revelation 1:17 and 22:13 apply this triple formula to the risen Christ: I am the first and the last and I am the Alpha and Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end. The MKT's exact rendering preserves the form that Revelation quotes.
13: My hand laid the foundation of the earth, my right hand spread out the heavens; when I summon them, they take their stand together. The creation claim as the basis for the identity claim of v12: the one who is first and last is also the creator of earth and heavens. The summoning of earth and heavens to stand together is the divine command over creation -- the creator's authority over what he made. The MKT's take their stand together renders ya'amdu yachdav: they stand as one at the summons.
14: YHWH is flagged -- The LORD loves him; he shall carry out his will against Babylon, and his arm shall be against the Chaldeans. The him is Cyrus (Isaiah 44:28; Isaiah 45:1) -- YHWH's chosen instrument. The divine love for the instrument is notable: not merely using Cyrus but loving him and ensuring he succeeds. The MKT's carry out his will renders ya'aseh cheftso: do/accomplish his desire/pleasure.
15: I, even I, have spoken; I have called him; I have brought him; and he will succeed in his mission. The emphatic I-statements (four first-person verbs) place the entirety of Cyrus's mission in the divine initiative: spoken, called, brought, and guaranteeing success. The MKT preserves the anaphora of the four verbs. Cyrus's success is already secured by the divine promise before it happens historically.
16: Ruach (Spirit, disp 4) is flagged -- And now the Lord GOD has sent me, and his Spirit. The speaker shifts to the first person -- either the prophet or the Servant. The claim that the Lord GOD has sent both the speaker and his Spirit creates a triad: the Lord GOD (sender), the speaker (sent), and the Spirit (also sent). The MKT's his Spirit capitalizes, reflecting the divine Spirit sent alongside the speaker. This verse has been interpreted as an early trinitarian formula: the LORD sending both the Son and the Spirit.
17: Elohim and YHWH are flagged twice -- I am the LORD your God, who teaches you what is good for you, who leads you in the way you should go. The double divine title (LORD your God) introduces the teaching-and-leading metaphor: YHWH as teacher and path-guide. The specificity -- teaches you what is good for you -- implies that YHWH's instruction is not arbitrary law but what genuinely benefits the people. The path-leading image is the Psalms 23:3 pattern.
18: Tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) and shalom (peace, disp 2) are flagged -- If only you had listened to my commandments! Then your peace would have been like a river, and your righteousness like the waves of the sea. The counterfactual lament: the peace that was available but not received. Shalom as a river -- the steady, broad, inexhaustible flow of covenantal wholeness. Tsedaqah as the waves of the sea -- the continuous, rhythmic, overwhelming rightness that should have characterized Israel's life. The MKT preserves the two similes without merging them.
19: Your descendants would have been like sand, your children like its grains; their name would never have been cut off or destroyed before me. The conditional blessing unrealized: the Abrahamic promise of sand-numbered descendants (Genesis 22:17) was available but forfeited through disobedience. The name that would never have been cut off contrasts with the real history of exile and dispersion. The lament of lost blessing is one of the most poignant moments in Second Isaiah.
20: YHWH is flagged -- the command to go out from Babylon and flee from Chaldea, declared with joy to the ends of the earth: the LORD has redeemed his servant Jacob. The redemption declaration is the gospel announcement (mevasser: herald of good news) that runs through Isaiah 40:9; Isaiah 52:7; Isaiah 61:1. The MKT's declare this with a cry of joy renders the commanded response: not quiet departure but joyful proclamation. The ends of the earth are the audience, giving the redemption of Jacob universal significance.
21: They did not thirst when he led them through the deserts -- he made water flow from the rock. The new Exodus recall: the miracle of the rock in the wilderness (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11) is cited as the pattern for the new return from Babylon. Paul identifies the rock as Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4): the spiritual rock that followed them was Christ. The MKT's rendering follows the Hebrew's past-tense narrative of what YHWH did -- a historical claim, not just a metaphor.
22: YHWH is flagged; shalom (peace, disp 2) is flagged -- There is no peace, says the LORD, for the wicked. The closing refrain of chapters 40-48 is a warning note: the redemption promises of the preceding chapters are not available to the wicked without repentance. The shalom that was a river (v18) is specifically denied to the resha'im (wicked). This refrain appears again at 57:21 as the closing statement of the second subsection (49-57), making it a structural marker of the book's sections.
